paragraphs.text:
\subsection{LLMs for Automated Scientific Research}
The use of large language models (LLMs) to automate or assist scientific research has attracted considerable attention. Luo et al.~\cite{llm4sr} present the first systematic survey mapping LLM applications across the full research cycle, including hypothesis discovery, experiment planning, scientific writing, and peer review. The AI Scientist~\cite{ai_scientist} introduces a fully automated, prompt-driven pipeline where frontier LLMs generate research ideas, write and execute code, produce experimental results, compose full papers, and perform simulated peer review—all at low computational cost. MLR-Copilot~\cite{mlr_copilot} proposes a three-phase LLM-agent framework combining IdeaAgent for hypothesis generation, ExperimentAgent for implementation, and iterative debugging with human feedback. Despite these advances, most existing systems rely on commercial, closed-source models as backends, which limits reproducibility and prevents the use of reinforcement learning (RL)-based policy optimization for iterative improvement. Si et al.~\cite{si_idea} conduct the first large-scale human study comparing LLM-generated and human-expert research ideas, finding that LLM ideas are rated as more novel but slightly less feasible, while also exposing critical limitations in LLM self-evaluation and generation diversity. These findings motivate the need for trainable, open-source models that can be refined through iterative feedback.

\subsection{LLMs for Automated Peer Review}
A parallel line of work investigates whether LLMs can assist or automate the peer review process. Liu and Shah~\cite{reviewergpt} provide an early exploratory study of GPT-4 for reviewing tasks, finding that LLMs show promise for error detection and checklist verification but struggle with holistic quality judgments. D'Arcy et al.~\cite{marg} propose MARG, a multi-agent review generation framework that distributes paper text across specialized LLM instances, enabling full-text processing beyond context-length limits and producing more specific, helpful feedback. Yu et al.~\cite{sea} introduce SEA, a standardized framework for automated peer review that normalizes heterogeneous reviews and incorporates self-correction via mismatch scoring. Tyser et al.~\cite{ai_driven_review} develop AI-driven review systems with bias-aware design, meta-prompting, and role-playing strategies, evaluating alignment with human preferences through pairwise comparisons. More recently, Zeng et al.~\cite{reviewrl} apply reinforcement learning to train review models with composite reward functions combining review quality and rating consistency, achieving improved factual grounding and analytical depth. Broader surveys on LLM-as-a-Judge~\cite{llm_judge_survey1,llm_judge_survey2} provide comprehensive taxonomies of using LLMs for evaluation, highlighting challenges in consistency, bias, and domain generalization. However, these approaches typically treat peer review as a standalone task rather than integrating it into a closed-loop system where review feedback directly drives iterative improvement of research output.

\subsection{Preference Optimization for LLM Alignment}
Aligning LLMs with human preferences has been a major focus of recent research. Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) traditionally requires training a separate reward model followed by policy optimization using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)~\cite{dpo} simplifies this by reparameterizing the reward function, enabling direct policy learning from preference pairs via a classification loss. Building on DPO, SimPO~\cite{simpo} introduces a reference-free reward based on the average log probability of sequences, eliminating the need for a reference model and improving alignment performance. Self-Rewarding Language Models~\cite{self_rewarding} propose an iterative framework where the LLM acts as its own judge, generating preference data for DPO training and improving both instruction-following and self-evaluation capabilities across iterations. Dong et al.~\cite{online_rlhf} present a practical workflow for online iterative RLHF using proxy preference models, demonstrating that iterative policy updates with freshly sampled data outperform offline approaches. Our work extends these ideas to the domain of scientific research by constructing a self-contained iterative loop where a reviewer model provides preference signals to optimize a researcher model, analogous to but distinct from general-purpose self-rewarding frameworks.

\subsection{LLM-Assisted Scientific Writing}
Beyond the full research pipeline, LLMs have also been applied to specific writing tasks in science. AutoSurvey~\cite{autosurvey} automates comprehensive literature survey generation through a multi-LLM pipeline with retrieval-augmented generation, addressing context-length limitations and factual hallucination risks. The broader survey by Luo et al.~\cite{llm4sr} systematically reviews LLM applications in scientific writing, including citation generation, abstract composition, and full-paper drafting. Our work differs by training models to generate complete research papers with structured outlines, simulated experimental results, and LaTeX formatting, conditioned on a corpus of references and their abstracts.

\subsection{AI-Generated Text Detection}
As LLM-generated content becomes increasingly prevalent, detecting machine-written text—particularly in academic settings—has emerged as a critical safeguard. Bao et al.~\cite{fast_detectgpt} propose Fast-DetectGPT, an efficient zero-shot detector based on conditional probability curvature that distinguishes human and machine-authored text without requiring training data or watermarking. This approach achieves substantial improvements in both accuracy and computational efficiency over prior perturbation-based methods such as DetectGPT~\cite{detectgpt}. We adopt Fast-DetectGPT as a component of our ethical safeguards to ensure transparency regarding the provenance of AI-generated research outputs.

s2_metadata.title: LLM4SR: A Survey on Large Language Models for Scientific Research

tldr.text: This is the first systematic survey on Large Language Models (LLMs) in scientific research, mapping their application across four core stages: hypothesis discovery (via literature-based and inductive reasoning), experiment planning (design and execution), automated scientific writing (citation, related work, drafting), and peer review. It analyzes task-specific methodologies, evaluation benchmarks, and identifies key challenges such as domain specificity, factual accuracy, and reproducibility, while proposing future directions including agentic reasoning and integration with domain-specific knowledge graphs.

references.raw_text: [2] Chris Lu, Cong Lu, Robert Tjarko Lange, Jakob Foerster, Jeff Clune, and David Ha. "The AI Scientist: Towards Fully Automated Open-Ended Scientific Discovery." 2024. arXiv:2408.06292.
references.title: The AI Scientist: Towards Fully Automated Open-Ended Scientific Discovery

s2_metadata.title: The AI Scientist: Towards Fully Automated Open-Ended Scientific Discovery

tldr.text: [research paper] THE AI SCIENTIST introduces the first end-to-end, fully automated framework for open-ended scientific discovery in machine learning, where frontier LLMs generate research ideas, design and execute experiments via code generation, produce visual and numerical results, write full scientific papers, and perform automated peer review—all at a cost of under $15 per paper. The system iteratively builds on prior discoveries, enabling scalable, low-cost, and self-improving scientific innovation, with demonstrated success across diffusion modeling, transformer architectures, and learning dynamics, and achieves near-human performance in paper evaluation through a foundation model-based reviewer.

references.title: MLR-Copilot: Autonomous Machine Learning Research based on Large Language Models Agents

s2_metadata.title: MLR-Copilot: Autonomous Machine Learning Research based on Large Language Models Agents

tldr.text: MLR-Copilot introduces a three-phase LLM-agent framework for autonomous machine learning research: (1) IdeaAgent generates hypothesis and experimental plans from research papers by extracting tasks, gaps, and keywords; (2) ExperimentAgent translates plans into executable code by retrieving prototype implementations, models, and data; (3) Execution phase runs experiments with iterative debugging and human feedback to refine results. The system demonstrates feasibility across five ML tasks, outperforming prior work by addressing full research cycles—including novelty, grounding in literature, and iterative validation—without relying on predefined templates or limited experimentation.

references.raw_text: [4] Chenglei Si, Diyi Yang, and Tatsunori Hashimoto. "Can LLMs Generate Novel Research Ideas? A Large-Scale Human Study with 100+ NLP Researchers." 2024. arXiv:2409.04109.
references.title: Can LLMs Generate Novel Research Ideas? A Large-Scale Human Study with 100+ NLP Researchers

s2_metadata.title: Can LLMs Generate Novel Research Ideas? A Large-Scale Human Study with 100+ NLP Researchers

tldr.text: [empirical study / human evaluation] A large-scale, controlled human study of 100+ NLP experts reveals that LLM-generated research ideas are statistically judged as more novel than human-generated ones (p < 0.05), despite slightly lower feasibility scores. The study identifies critical limitations in current LLM ideation agents, including poor self-evaluation, lack of idea diversity under scaling, and susceptibility to overgeneration bias; it also proposes an end-to-end execution study to validate whether novelty translates into actual research outcomes.

references.raw_text: [5] Ryan Liu and Nihar B. Shah. "ReviewerGPT? An Exploratory Study on Using Large Language Models for Paper Reviewing." 2023. arXiv:2306.00622.
references.title: ReviewerGPT? An Exploratory Study on Using Large Language Models for Paper Reviewing

s2_metadata.title: ReviewerGPT? An Exploratory Study on Using Large Language Models for Paper Reviewing

tldr.text: [exploratory study] Large language models (LLMs), specifically GPT-4, show promise in targeted peer review tasks: they correctly identify errors in 7/13 papers (mathematical and logical), achieve 86.6% accuracy in verifying author checklists against ground truth, and detect factual inconsistencies in paper responses; however, they fail to reliably select the better of two abstracts—erring in 6/10 cases due to biases toward positive results, misinterpretations of bounds, and susceptibility to buzzwords or prompt injection—indicating strong utility in structured verification but limited capability in holistic, judgment-based evaluation.

references.raw_text: [6] Mike D'Arcy, Tom Hope, Larry Birnbaum, and Doug Downey. "MARG: Multi-Agent Review Generation for Scientific Papers." 2024. arXiv:2401.04259.
references.title: MARG: Multi-Agent Review Generation for Scientific Papers

s2_metadata.title: MARG: Multi-Agent Review Generation for Scientific Papers

tldr.text: [research paper] MARG introduces a multi-agent framework that overcomes LLM input length limits by distributing scientific paper text across specialized GPT-4 agents, enabling full-text reasoning. Agents engage in internal dialogue to generate feedback on experiments, clarity, and impact, resulting in 2.2x more specific and helpful comments than single-agent baselines—reducing generic feedback from 60% to 29% and increasing good comments from 1.7 to 3.7 per paper—while maintaining accuracy despite communication overhead and cost challenges.

s2_metadata.title: Automated Peer Reviewing in Paper SEA: Standardization, Evaluation, and Analysis

tldr.text: [research paper] SEA introduces a three-module framework for automated scientific paper reviewing: (1) SEA-S standardizes heterogeneous peer reviews into unified, constructive feedback using GPT-4 distillation and Mistral-7B fine-tuning; (2) SEA-E generates high-quality reviews by leveraging standardized inputs and human-crafted prompts via SFT; (3) SEA-A introduces a mismatch score to quantify content inconsistency between papers and reviews, enabling a self-correction loop that regenerates reviews when discrepancies are detected. The framework achieves superior comprehensiveness, consistency, and constructiveness across eight venues through structured data integration and iterative refinement.

s2_metadata.title: AI-Driven Review Systems: Evaluating LLMs in Scalable and Bias-Aware Academic Reviews

tldr.text: [research paper] The paper presents a proof-of-concept for AI-driven peer review systems using large language models (LLMs), evaluating their alignment with human reviews through pairwise preference comparisons and automated preference prediction. It introduces three systems—OpenReviewer for automated reviews, Papers with Reviews as a public platform, and Reviewer Arena for preference-based evaluation—and employs meta-prompting, role-playing, and multi-agent dialogue to enhance review quality. The work identifies and mitigates LLM review limitations via error injection, bias detection, and adherence to ethical guidelines, while enabling scalable, bias-aware, and transparent review processes for academic papers.

references.raw_text: [9] Sihang Zeng et al. "ReviewRL: Towards Automated Scientific Review with RL." 2025. arXiv:2508.10308.
references.title: ReviewRL: Towards Automated Scientific Review with RL

s2_metadata.title: ReviewRL: Towards Automated Scientific Review with RL

tldr.text: [research paper] ReviewRL introduces a reinforcement learning framework for automated scientific peer review that combines ArXiv-MCP retrieval-augmented context generation, supervised fine-tuning for foundational review capabilities, and a composite reward function that jointly optimizes review quality and rating consistency. Unlike rule-based reward methods, it integrates judge-model-based evaluations to ensure factual grounding, analytical depth, and alignment with human judgments, achieving superior performance in context-awareness, factual consistency, and review depth across ICLR 2025 papers.

references.raw_text: [10] Dawei Li et al. "From Generation to Judgment: Opportunities and Challenges of LLM-as-a-judge." 2024. arXiv:2411.16594.
references.title: From Generation to Judgment: Opportunities and Challenges of LLM-as-a-judge

s2_metadata.title: From Generation to Judgment: Opportunities and Challenges of LLM-as-a-judge

tldr.text: [survey paper] This survey presents a systematic taxonomy of LLM-as-a-judge, defining it through input/output formats and categorizing judgment tasks across three dimensions: (1) attributes (helpfulness, safety, reliability, relevance, logical consistency, overall quality), (2) methodologies (prompting, fine-tuning, preference learning, multi-agent collaboration, etc.), and (3) benchmarks (general performance, bias, challenging tasks, domain-specific). It highlights the shift from static metrics to human-like, fine-grained, scalable evaluations in ML/NLP, while identifying critical challenges like bias, vulnerability, and lack of standardized evaluation frameworks.

references.raw_text: [11] Jiawei Gu et al. "A Survey on LLM-as-a-Judge." 2024. arXiv:2411.15594.
references.title: A Survey on LLM-as-a-Judge

s2_metadata.title: A Survey on LLM-as-a-Judge

tldr.text: [survey paper] This survey establishes a formal taxonomy of LLM-as-a-Judge systems, defining them as LLMs that assess whether inputs conform to given rules or standards, and systematically categorizes methods by evaluation scope, input modality, and reasoning mechanism. It identifies key reliability challenges—consistency, bias, and scalability—and proposes a novel benchmark to evaluate these, emphasizing strategies like prompt engineering, dynamic feedback loops, and cross-model validation to improve judgment fidelity in diverse domains such as academic review, code quality, and multimodal content assessment.

references.raw_text: [12] Rafael Rafailov, Archit Sharma, Eric Mitchell, Stefano Ermon, Christopher D. Manning, and Chelsea Finn. "Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model." 2023. arXiv:2305.18290.
references.title: Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model

s2_metadata.title: Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model

tldr.text: [research paper] Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) replaces reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) with a closed-form, binary cross-entropy objective that directly optimizes language model policies to align with human preferences. By reparameterizing the RLHF objective using a change of variables, DPO eliminates the need for reward modeling, sampling, or complex policy updates—simplifying training while achieving performance comparable to or exceeding PPO-based RLHF in sentiment control, summarization, and dialogue tasks, with superior stability and computational efficiency.

references.raw_text: [13] Yu Meng, Mengzhou Xia, and Danqi Chen. "SimPO: Simple Preference Optimization with a Reference-Free Reward." 2024. arXiv:2405.14734.
references.title: SimPO: Simple Preference Optimization with a Reference-Free Reward

s2_metadata.title: SimPO: Simple Preference Optimization with a Reference-Free Reward

tldr.text: [research paper] SimPO introduces a reference-free preference optimization method that uses the average log probability of a response under the policy model as the implicit reward, aligning training with generation metrics. This eliminates the need for a reference model and improves performance by up to 7.5 points on Arena-Hard and 6.4 points on AlpacaEval 2 over DPO, while maintaining minimal response length growth and outperforming state-of-the-art models in real-user evaluations.

references.raw_text: [14] Weizhe Yuan, Richard Yuanzhe Pang, Kyunghyun Cho, Xian Li, Sainbayar Sukhbaatar, Jing Xu, and Jason Weston. "Self-Rewarding Language Models." 2024. arXiv:2401.10020.
references.title: Self-Rewarding Language Models

s2_metadata.title: Self-Rewarding Language Models

tldr.text: [research paper] Self-Rewarding Language Models introduce an iterative alignment framework where a language model simultaneously improves instruction-following and self-generated reward modeling capabilities by acting as its own judge via LLM-as-a-Judge prompting. The model generates new instruction-response pairs and evaluates them internally, creating a self-improving preference dataset used in DPO training, leading to superior performance on AlpacaEval 2.0 compared to baseline models and existing systems like Claude 2, Gemini Pro, and GPT-4 0613, with both generation and reward quality improving across iterations.

references.raw_text: [15] Hanze Dong et al. "RLHF Workflow: From Reward Modeling to Online RLHF." 2024. arXiv:2405.07863.
references.title: RLHF Workflow: From Reward Modeling to Online RLHF

s2_metadata.title: RLHF Workflow: From Reward Modeling to Online RLHF

tldr.text: [technical report] This paper presents a practical, fully open-source workflow for online iterative Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) that overcomes the infeasibility of real-time human feedback by constructing proxy preference models from diverse open-source datasets. It formalizes the RLHF process using the Bradley-Terry model and KL-regularized policy optimization, then implements a scalable, reproducible pipeline that achieves state-of-the-art performance on LLM benchmarks like AlpacaEval-2, Arena-Hard, and HumanEval without closed-source data or human annotations. The method enables open-source communities to replicate high-performance alignment via iterative policy updates driven by learned preference signals.

references.raw_text: [16] Yidong Wang et al. "AutoSurvey: Large Language Models Can Automatically Write Surveys." 2024. arXiv:2406.10252.
references.title: AutoSurvey: Large Language Models Can Automatically Write Surveys

s2_metadata.title: AutoSurvey: Large Language Models Can Automatically Write Surveys

tldr.text: [research paper] AutoSurvey introduces a two-stage, multi-LLM pipeline to automate comprehensive literature surveys in fast-evolving domains like LLMs, overcoming context limits and hallucination risks via parallel outline generation, retrieval-augmented content updates, and a multi-LLM evaluation framework. It uses RAG for real-time knowledge injection during drafting, ensures document consistency through systematic revision, and validates output quality via a multi-LLM-as-judge evaluation system trained on high-quality survey corpora, achieving a 3-minute generation time with $1.2 cost per survey.

references.raw_text: [17] Guangsheng Bao, Yanbin Zhao, Zhiyang Teng, Linyi Yang, and Yue Zhang. "Fast-DetectGPT: Efficient Zero-Shot Detection of Machine-Generated Text via Conditional Probability Curvature." 2023. arXiv:2310.05130.
references.title: Fast-DetectGPT: Efficient Zero-Shot Detection of Machine-Generated Text via Conditional Probability Curvature

s2_metadata.title: Fast-DetectGPT: Efficient Zero-Shot Detection of Machine-Generated Text via Conditional Probability Curvature

tldr.text: [research paper] Fast-DetectGPT introduces a novel zero-shot detection framework based on conditional probability curvature, which captures the distinct token selection patterns between humans and machines. Instead of perturbing text via expensive model calls (as in DetectGPT), it efficiently samples alternative word choices and evaluates their conditional probabilities, reducing computational cost by 340x while improving detection accuracy by ~75% in both white-box and black-box settings, with superior performance in black-box scenarios where it outperforms DetectGPT's white-box results by 28%.

references.raw_text: [18] Eric Mitchell et al. "DetectGPT: Zero-Shot Machine-Generated Text Detection using Probability Curvature." 2023. arXiv:2301.11305.
references.title: DetectGPT: Zero-Shot Machine-Generated Text Detection using Probability Curvature

s2_metadata.title: DetectGPT: Zero-Shot Machine-Generated Text Detection using Probability Curvature

tldr.text: [research paper] DetectGPT leverages the negative curvature property of large language models' log probability functions—where LLM-generated text tends to reside in regions of negative curvature (e.g., local maxima)—to detect machine-generated text without training, datasets, or watermarking. It computes the average log probability of a passage under the source model versus perturbed versions generated by a generic model (e.g., T5), using the log probability ratio to identify if the original text lies in a high-curvature region typical of LLM outputs. This curvature-based approach outperforms existing zero-shot baselines, achieving 0.95 AUROC in detecting GPT-NeoX-generated fake news versus 0.81 for prior methods.

parsed_ground_truth.text:
LLMs for Research.
In recent years, several studies have explored using language models for creative tasks in research, such as multi-agent collaborative writing (Baek et al., 2024) and multi-module retrieval (Yang et al., 2023) to improve research idea generation. These works aim to boost the novelty and diversity of AI in creative tasks. Si et al. (2024) conducted a comprehensive human evaluation of the task of idea generation by language models. Wang et al. (2024b) proposed using LLMs to automatically write survey papers. Additionally, LLMs have been used to automate the research process: Huang et al. (2024) introduced a benchmark for evaluating LLMs in coding solutions for machine learning problems; Wang et al. (2023b) proposed a method leveraging LLMs for scientific literature retrieval. The AI Scientist project (Lu et al., 2024) introduced a fully automated, prompt-driven research pipeline. However, prompt-based methods often fail to generate ideas that are both diverse and practical, limiting their real-world application. To address this, we developed an iterative self-rewarding framework that enables the LLM to refine its ideas continuously, enhancing both diversity and practicality in research proposal generation.

LLMs for Science Discovery.
The tradition of Al-assisted scientific discovery (Langley, 1987; 2024) has a long history. As early as the last century, Al was applied in fields such as chemistry (Buchanan & Feigenbaum, 1981), synthetic biology (Jumper et al., 2021; Hayes et al., 2024), material discovery (Pyzer-Knapp et al., 2022; Merchant et al., 2023), and mathematics (Romera-Paredes et al., 2024). With the development of neural networks (LeCun et al., 2015), more researchers have focused on AI4Science (A14Science & Quantum, 2023; LI, 2024; Yakaboski et al., 2023). Al is mainly used for data analysis within a single domain, playing a passive role without driving scientific discovery. The key challenge is enabling Al to go beyond analysis and actively contribute to generating new research ideas, which demands advanced reasoning and creativity. Our work builds on Al's historical role in science, aiming to shift Al from a supporting tool to a leader in scientific discovery.

Automated Evaluation of Research Papers.
The use of AI tools in the scientific publishing process has garnered widespread attention (Bao et al., 2021; Liu & Shah, 2023; Liang et al., 2024; D'Arcy et al., 2024), including summarizing research paper content (Collins et al., 2017), detecting inaccuracies (Nuijten et al., 2016), and identifying fairness disparities (Zhang et al., 2022). Hosseini & Horbach (2023) conducted small-scale qualitative experiments to evaluate the effectiveness of ChatGPT in the peer review process, while Robertson (2023) invited 10 participants to assess the benefits of GPT-4 in assisting with peer review. Lu et al. (2024) and Tyser et al. (2024) used GPT-4 to evaluate full-text PDFs of scientific papers. However, when LLMs act as judges, even the most advanced models, such as GPT-4 (Achiam et al., 2023) and Gemini (Reid et al., 2024), still lag behind reward models specifically trained for the task, as seen in RewardBench (Lambert et al., 2024). This gap highlights the challenge of achieving human-level judgment and reasoning in AI-driven peer reviews. In contrast, we train a Generative Reward Model (Zhang et al., 2024) to simulate a comprehensive peer review. Our CycleReviewer simulates reviewers with varying perspectives, documenting summaries, strengths, and weaknesses. In the final stage, a primary reviewer consolidates these insights to deliver the final decision.

s2_metadata.title: AI for Open Science: A Multi-Agent Perspective for Ethically Translating Data to Knowledge

tldr.text: [research paper] The paper introduces AI for Open Science (AI4OS) as a multi-agent extension of AI4Science, formalizing openness through a Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD)-based language that models collaborative knowledge translation across disjoint scientific agents. It proposes a MaDiSS framework with three key stages—prior knowledge communication, pattern sharing between agents, and collective knowledge review—and introduces a theoretical openness metric grounded in KDD stages and ethical principles to ensure equitable, transparent, and socially beneficial outcomes in self-driving labs and AI-driven research systems.

references.raw_text: Yang, Zonglin, Du, Xinya, Li, Junxian, Zheng, Jie, Poria, Soujanya, and Cambria, Erik. Large language models for automated open-domain scientific hypotheses discovery. arXiv preprint arXiv:2309.02726, 2023.
references.title: Large Language Models for Automated Open-domain Scientific Hypotheses Discovery

s2_metadata.title: Large Language Models for Automated Open-domain Scientific Hypotheses Discovery

tldr.text: [research paper] Introduces TOMATO (Automated Open-domain Hypothetical Induction), a novel NLP task where LLMs generate novel, valid scientific hypotheses from raw web corpora without manual selection or commonsense priors. Proposes the first dataset for social science hypothesis discovery using 50 recent journal papers, with raw web passages as input. Develops MOOSE, a multi-module LLM framework with three feedback mechanisms—present, past, and future feedback—that iteratively refine hypothesis generation, outperforming baseline LLMs in both GPT-4 and expert evaluations by enabling self-critique, retrospective validation, and cross-module justification.

references.raw_text: Zhang, Jiayao, Zhang, Hongming, Deng, Zhun, and Roth, Dan. Investigating fairness disparities in peer review: A language model enhanced approach. arXiv preprint arXiv:2211.06398, 2022.
references.title: Investigating Fairness Disparities in Peer Review: A Language Model Enhanced Approach

s2_metadata.title: Investigating Fairness Disparities in Peer Review: A Language Model Enhanced Approach

tldr.text: [research paper] The paper presents a comprehensive dataset of ICLR peer reviews (2017–2022) aggregated from OpenReview, arXiv, and CSRanking, enriched with high-level textual features extracted via large language models (LLMs). It evaluates fairness disparities across author gender, geography, and institutional prestige using demographic parity, equalized odds, and AUC difference metrics, finding that textual features significantly improve predictive accuracy and reduce bias compared to baseline models. The study reveals that while disparities exist (e.g., North American dominance), they are not statistically compelling; however, the review generation and scoring tasks remain challenging for current LMs, highlighting the need for new NLP methods in automated peer review.

references.raw_text: Zhang, Lunjun, Hosseini, Arian, Bansal, Hritik, Kazemi, Mehran, Kumar, Aviral, and Agarwal, Rishabh. Generative verifiers: Reward modeling as next-token prediction. arXiv preprint arXiv:2408.15240, 2024.
references.title: Generative Verifiers: Reward Modeling as Next-Token Prediction

s2_metadata.title: Generative Verifiers: Reward Modeling as Next-Token Prediction

tldr.text: [research paper] The paper proposes Generative Reward Models (GenRM) trained via next-token prediction instead of discriminative scoring, enabling LLMs to perform reasoning-based verification through chain-of-thought (CoT) rationales. GenRM-CoT explicitly generates verification rationales before predicting correctness, allowing for majority voting at test time to improve accuracy—outperforming discriminative RMs, LLM-as-a-Judge, and self-consistency by 20–30 percentage points on GSM8K and MATH/MMLU, with significant gains in error detection for subtle logical flaws (e.g., misreading 'each' in pricing problems). The method leverages inherent generative capabilities of LLMs for unified instruction tuning, CoT reasoning, and scalable verification via test-time inference.

topics.label: LLMs for Research
topics.summary: Summarizes works using LLMs for creative research tasks, including multi-agent writing, retrieval, idea generation, automated surveys, and benchmarking. Identifies limitation of prompt-based methods and proposes an iterative self-rewarding framework.

topics.label: LLMs for Science Discovery
topics.summary: Reviews history of AI-assisted scientific discovery in various domains, noting that AI is mainly used for passive data analysis. Highlights the challenge of enabling AI to actively generate new research ideas.

topics.label: Automated Evaluation of Research Papers
topics.summary: Discusses AI tools for scientific publishing, including summarizing, detecting inaccuracies, and fairness. Highlights that LLM judges underperform compared to specialized reward models (RewardBench). Proposes training a Generative Reward Model and CycleReviewer for comprehensive peer review.

Si et al. conducted a comprehensive human evaluation of LLM-generated research ideas.

Prompt-based methods often fail to generate ideas that are both diverse and practical, limiting real-world application.
Developed an iterative self-rewarding framework that enables the LLM to refine its ideas continuously, enhancing both diversity and practicality.
The tradition of AI-assisted scientific discovery dates back to early applications in chemistry, biology, materials, and mathematics.
AI is mainly used for data analysis within a single domain, playing a passive role without driving scientific discovery.
The key challenge is enabling AI to go beyond analysis and actively contribute to generating new research ideas, which demands advanced reasoning and creativity.
Our work builds on AI's historical role in science, aiming to shift AI from a supporting tool to a leader in scientific discovery.
AI tools are used in scientific publishing for summarizing content, detecting inaccuracies, and identifying fairness disparities.
Hosseini & Horbach conducted small-scale qualitative experiments to evaluate ChatGPT in peer review.
Robertson invited 10 participants to assess GPT-4's benefits in peer review assistance.

When LLMs act as judges, even advanced models like GPT-4 and Gemini lag behind reward models trained specifically for the task, as shown in RewardBench.
This gap highlights the challenge of achieving human-level judgment and reasoning in AI-driven peer reviews.
We train a Generative Reward Model (Zhang et al., 2024) to simulate a comprehensive peer review.
CycleReviewer simulates reviewers with varying perspectives, documenting summaries, strengths, and weaknesses, then a primary reviewer consolidates for final decision.

topics.label: LLMs for Automated Scientific Research
topics.summary: Surveys and systems that use LLMs to automate scientific research, including idea generation, experimentation, and paper writing. Highlights limitations of closed-source models and the need for open-source, trainable models.

topics.label: LLMs for Automated Peer Review
topics.summary: Explores the use of LLMs for automating peer review, including review generation, standardization, bias mitigation, and RL-based training. Notes the lack of integrated closed-loop systems.

topics.label: Preference Optimization for LLM Alignment
topics.summary: Methods for aligning LLMs with human preferences, including RLHF, DPO, SimPO, Self-Rewarding, and online RLHF. The paper extends these to scientific research.

topics.label: LLM-Assisted Scientific Writing
topics.summary: LLM applications for scientific writing tasks, including automatic survey generation and full paper drafting. The paper's approach generates complete papers with structured outlines and LaTeX.

topics.label: AI-Generated Text Detection
topics.summary: Detection of machine-generated text, emphasizing Fast-DetectGPT as an efficient zero-shot method. The paper uses it for ethical safeguards.

claims.text: Luo et al. present the first systematic survey mapping LLM applications across the full research cycle.

The AI Scientist introduces a fully automated, prompt-driven pipeline where frontier LLMs generate research ideas, write and execute code, produce experimental results, compose full papers, and perform simulated peer review at low computational cost.
MLR-Copilot proposes a three-phase LLM-agent framework combining IdeaAgent for hypothesis generation, ExperimentAgent for implementation, and iterative debugging with human feedback.
Most existing systems rely on commercial, closed-source models, which limits reproducibility and prevents the use of RL-based policy optimization for iterative improvement.

Si et al. conduct the first large-scale human study comparing LLM-generated and human-expert research ideas, finding LLM ideas are rated as more novel but less feasible, and exposing limitations in LLM self-evaluation and generation diversity.
These findings motivate the need for trainable, open-source models that can be refined through iterative feedback.
Liu and Shah provide an early exploratory study of GPT-4 for reviewing tasks, finding LLMs show promise for error detection and checklist verification but struggle with holistic quality judgments.

D'Arcy et al. propose MARG, a multi-agent review generation framework that distributes paper text across specialized LLM instances enabling full-text processing beyond context-length limits and producing more specific feedback.
Yu et al. introduce SEA, a standardized framework for automated peer review that normalizes heterogeneous reviews and incorporates self-correction via mismatch scoring.
Tyser et al. develop AI-driven review systems with bias-aware design, meta-prompting, and role-playing strategies, evaluating alignment with human preferences through pairwise comparisons.

Zeng et al. apply reinforcement learning to train review models with composite reward functions combining review quality and rating consistency, achieving improved factual grounding and analytical depth.
Broader surveys on LLM-as-a-Judge provide comprehensive taxonomies of using LLMs for evaluation, highlighting challenges in consistency, bias, and domain generalization.
These approaches typically treat peer review as a standalone task rather than integrating it into a closed-loop system where review feedback directly drives iterative improvement of research output.
Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) traditionally requires training a separate reward model followed by policy optimization using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO).
Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) simplifies RLHF by reparameterizing the reward function, enabling direct policy learning from preference pairs via a classification loss.
SimPO introduces a reference-free reward based on the average log probability of sequences, eliminating the need for a reference model and improving alignment performance.

Self-Rewarding Language Models propose an iterative framework where the LLM acts as its own judge, generating preference data for DPO training and improving both instruction-following and self-evaluation capabilities across iterations.
Dong et al. present a practical workflow for online iterative RLHF using proxy preference models, demonstrating that iterative policy updates with freshly sampled data outperform offline approaches.
Our work extends these ideas to the domain of scientific research by constructing a self-contained iterative loop where a reviewer model provides preference signals to optimize a researcher model.

AutoSurvey automates comprehensive literature survey generation through a multi-LLM pipeline with retrieval-augmented generation, addressing context-length limitations and factual hallucination risks.
The broader survey by Luo et al. systematically reviews LLM applications in scientific writing, including citation generation, abstract composition, and full-paper drafting.

Our work differs by training models to generate complete research papers with structured outlines, simulated experimental results, and LaTeX formatting, conditioned on a corpus of references and their abstracts.

Bao et al. propose Fast-DetectGPT, an efficient zero-shot detector based on conditional probability curvature that distinguishes human and machine-authored text without requiring training data or watermarking.
Fast-DetectGPT achieves substantial improvements in both accuracy and computational efficiency over prior perturbation-based methods such as DetectGPT.
We adopt Fast-DetectGPT as a component of our ethical safeguards to ensure transparency regarding the provenance of AI-generated research outputs.

topic_alignment.rationale: GTopic1 covers LLMs for creative research tasks including multi-agent writing, idea generation, surveys, and benchmarking, as well as a self-rewarding framework. STopic1 (Automated Scientific Research) matches the automation of research tasks but is broader, STopic3 (Preference Optimization) aligns with the self-rewarding component, and STopic4 (Scientific Writing) directly addresses writing and survey generation. The candidate collectively covers much of GTopic1 but misses explicit benchmarking and multi-agent aspects, leading to a slightly reduced score.

GTopic2 focuses on the history of AI-assisted scientific discovery and the challenge of active idea generation. STopic1 (Automated Scientific Research) partially aligns through its mention of idea generation and experimentation, but lacks the historical perspective and depth of passive vs. active analysis. Thus a moderate match.

GTopic3 discusses automated evaluation of research papers, including peer review, summarization, and fairness, with a proposal for a Generative Reward Model. STopic2 (Automated Peer Review) directly addresses peer review automation, standardization, and bias mitigation. However, GTopic3's broader scope (e.g., summarization, statistical checking) is not fully covered, resulting in a high but not perfect score.

While this provides detailed coverage, it slightly reduces cohesiveness and could benefit from merging some overlapping topics (e.g., S2 and S5).

topic_ordering.rationale: The candidate ordering starts with automation (S2), then peer review (S3), preference optimization (S4), writing (S5), and detection (S6). This flow is somewhat logical but jumps from broad automation to specific evaluation to methodology to writing to safeguards. A more coherent order might group S2 and S5 together, place S4 after S3 (since alignment relates to evaluation), and move S6 to a separate section. The gold ordering (research, discovery, evaluation) is more topically progressive.

synthesis_quality.rationale: The candidate organizes the related work into clear subsections and does not merely list papers. It identifies key limitations (e.g., reliance on closed-source models, lack of closed-loop integration) and draws connections to motivate the proposed approach. However, the synthesis is somewhat shallow in parts: there is limited comparative analysis among methods within the same subtopic (e.g., comparing different automated research systems beyond brief descriptions), and the sections (especially the last two) feel less integrated with the main narrative. The candidate also misses the historical context of AI-assisted scientific discovery highlighted in the gold standard, which would strengthen the landscape building.

Some subsections (especially preference optimization) read more as background than as critical synthesis of the state of the art.

writing_quality.rationale: The writing maintains a professional academic tone with consistent terminology and clear sentence structures. Transitions between sentences are generally smooth, and the use of citations is accurate. The text is concise for the most part, though a few sentences are slightly verbose. Minor issues include the repetition of Luo et al. in two separate sections and the somewhat abrupt introduction of the detection section. Overall, the writing is strong and effectively communicates the literature review.
issues: Repetition of Luo et al. citation in both the first and fourth subsections.
The last subsection on AI-generated text detection feels tacked on, lacking a transition or integration with the previous content.

Some sentences could be more concise (e.g., 'The AI Scientist introduces a fully automated, prompt-driven pipeline where frontier LLMs generate research ideas, write and execute code, produce experimental results, compose full papers, and perform simulated peer review—all at low computational cost' is a bit long).

topic_alignment.rationale: GTopic1 covers LLMs for creative research tasks including multi-agent writing, idea generation, surveys, and benchmarking, as well as a self-rewarding framework. STopic1 (Automated Scientific Research) matches the automation of research tasks but is broader, STopic3 (Preference Optimization) aligns with the self-rewarding component, and STopic4 (Scientific Writing) directly addresses writing and survey generation. The candidate collectively covers much of GTopic1 but misses explicit benchmarking and multi-agent aspects, leading to a slightly reduced score.

GTopic2 focuses on the history of AI-assisted scientific discovery and the challenge of active idea generation. STopic1 (Automated Scientific Research) partially aligns through its mention of idea generation and experimentation, but lacks the historical perspective and depth of passive vs. active analysis. Thus a moderate match.

GTopic3 discusses automated evaluation of research papers, including peer review, summarization, and fairness, with a proposal for a Generative Reward Model. STopic2 (Automated Peer Review) directly addresses peer review automation, standardization, and bias mitigation. However, GTopic3's broader scope (e.g., summarization, statistical checking) is not fully covered, resulting in a high but not perfect score.

details.rationale: The candidate organizes the related work into clear subsections and does not merely list papers. It identifies key limitations (e.g., reliance on closed-source models, lack of closed-loop integration) and draws connections to motivate the proposed approach. The candidate also misses the historical context of AI-assisted scientific discovery highlighted in the gold standard, which would strengthen the landscape building.
